critique.summary: The article claims that the open problem related to improving lower bounds on a sequence H(n) in Ramsey theory has been solved using GPT-5.4 Pro, with confirmation from a contributing mathematician, Will Brian. The solution is planned to be written up for publication, and other AI models have also solved the problem using a developed testing scaffold.
mainPoints: Kevin Barreto and Liam Price used GPT-5.4 Pro to find a solution to a frontier math open problem involving hypergraphs and Ramsey theory.
Will Brian, a contributor to the problem, confirmed the solution and expressed interest in publishing it and exploring follow-on work.
Other AI models such as Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4 (xhigh) also solved the problem using a general testing scaffold.
The problem involves improving lower bounds on H(n), related to partitions in hypergraphs, which has been a challenging open problem in mathematics.
The solution and related AI interactions are documented and will be updated with future publications.
critique.truthfulness: The claim that GPT-5.4 Pro solved a known open problem in Ramsey theory is extraordinary and currently lacks independent verification from established mathematical journals or multiple independent expert sources. While the article states that a contributing mathematician confirmed the solution and plans to publish it, there is no evidence yet of peer-reviewed publication or broader community acceptance. The involvement of multiple AI models in solving the problem is plausible given recent advances in AI-assisted mathematics, but the claim should be treated cautiously until formal publication and peer review occur.
considerations: The article does not provide direct links to the full solution or peer-reviewed publications verifying the claim.
No independent expert opinions or external validations beyond the contributor Will Brian are presented.
The complexity and significance of the problem suggest that independent verification by the mathematical community is necessary before accepting the claim.
The article does not discuss potential limitations or errors in the AI-generated solution.
The broader implications for AI in mathematical research and the reproducibility of the solution by other researchers are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the recent claim that GPT-5.4 Pro and other frontier AI models have solved a previously open problem in mathematics, sparking debate on AI's ability to generate novel ideas and the implications for the future of research and AI development. Commenters express a mix of amazement, skepticism, and curiosity about the novelty and significance of the achievement, as well as the transparency and reproducibility of the results.

comments.author: qnleigh
comments.text:
I am kind of amazed at how many commenters respond to this result by confidently asserting that LLMs will never generate 'truly novel' ideas or problem solutions.

> AI is a remixer; it remixes all known ideas together. It won't come up with new ideas

> it's not because the model is figuring out something new

> LLMs will NEVER be able to do that, because it doesn't exist

It's not enough to say 'it will never be able to do X because it's not in the training data,' because we have countless counterexamples to this statement (e.g. 167,383 * 426,397 = 71,371,609,051, or the above announcement). You need to say why it can do some novel tasks but could never do others. And it should be clear why this post or others like it don't contradict your argument.

If you have been making these kinds of arguments against LLMs and acknowledge that novelty lies on a continuum, I am really curious why you draw the line where you do. And most importantly, what evidence would change your mind?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on AI novelty, challenging simplistic claims and encouraging evidence-based discussion, making it a strong and balanced contribution.

comments.author: vlinx
comments.text: This is a remarkable result if confirmed independently. The gap between solving competition problems and open research problems has always been significant - bridging that gap suggests something qualitatively different in the model capabilities.
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of independent verification and the significance of AI solving open research problems, reflecting a realistic and informed viewpoint.

comments.author: EternalFury
comments.text:
I am thinking there’s a large category of problems that can be solved by resampling existing proofs.
It’s the kind of brute force expedition machine can attempt relentlessly where humans would go mad trying.
It probably doesn’t really advance the field, but it can turn conjectures into theorems.
comments.analysis: Offers a plausible explanation of how AI might solve problems through extensive search rather than deep insight, contributing a valuable critical angle.

comments.author: zurfer
comments.text:
"In this scaffold, several other models were able to solve the problem as well: Opus 4.6 (max), Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4 (xhigh)."

I find that very surprising. This problem seems out of reach 3 months ago but now the 3 frontier models are able to solve it.

Is everybody distilling each others models? Companies sell the same data and RL environment to all big labs? Anybody more involved can share some rumors? :P

I do believe that AI can solve hard problems, but that progress is so distributed in a narrow domain makes me a bit suspicious somehow that there is a hidden factor. Like did some "data worker" solve a problem like that and it's now in the training data?
comments.analysis: Raises reasonable questions about data sharing and training overlaps that could influence results, encouraging transparency and scrutiny.

comments.author: Validark
comments.text: I have long said I am an AI doubter until AI could print out the answers to hard problems or ones requiring tons of innovation. Assuming this is verified to be correct (not by AI) then I just became a believer. I would like to see a few more AI inventions to know for sure, but wow, it really is a new and exciting world. I really hope we use this intelligence resource to make the world better.
comments.analysis: Expresses a genuine shift in belief based on the reported achievement, reflecting common public sentiment but dependent on verification.

comments.author: alberth
comments.text:
For those, like me, who find the prompt itself of interest …

> A full transcript of the original conversation with GPT-5.4 Pro can be found here [0] and GPT-5.4 Pro’s write-up from the end of that transcript can be found here [1].

[0] https://epoch.ai/files/open-problems/gpt-5-4-pro-hypergraph-...

[1] https://epoch.ai/files/open-problems/hypergraph-ramsey-gpt-5...
comments.analysis: Provides useful resources to validate and understand the claim, supporting transparency and deeper investigation.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
I was trying to get Claude and Codex to try and write a proof in Isabelle for the Collatz conjecture, but annoyingly it didn't solve it, and I don't feel like I'm any closer than I was when I started. AI is useless!

In all seriousness, this is pretty cool. I suspect that there's a lot of theoretical math that haven't been solved simply because of the "size" of the proof. An AI feedback loop into something like Isabelle or Lean does seem like it could end up opening up a lot of proofs.
comments.analysis: Insightfully discusses current AI limitations and potential future integration with formal proof systems, showing practical understanding.

comments.author: pugio
comments.text:
I've never yet been "that guy" on HN but... the title seems misleading. The actual title is "A Ramsey-style Problem on Hypergraphs" and a more descriptive title would be "All latest frontier models can solve a frontier math open problem". (It wasn't just GPT 5.4)

Super cool, of course.
comments.analysis: Corrects a common misconception in the headline, emphasizing accuracy in reporting while maintaining enthusiasm.

comments.author: jvdsf
comments.text: Really? He was steering the wheel the whole time. GPT didn't do the math.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism but lacks evidence; underestimates the capabilities demonstrated by the model and the collaborative nature of AI-assisted research.

comments.author: anematode
comments.text:
Fantastic and exciting stuff!

I wonder how much of this meteoric progress in actually creating novel mathematics is because the training data is of a much higher standard than code, for example.
comments.analysis: Makes a reasonable hypothesis about data quality influencing AI performance, though it remains speculative without direct evidence.
